mergeRationale: The patch swaps the newline-tolerant $ anchor for \Z in both username validators, so trailing-newline usernames are rejected while all previously valid input is untouched, and it adds negative regression tests that fail on the old regex.

requiredFindings.summary: In Python regex, $ can match before a trailing newline while \Z only matches at the absolute end of the string, so 'joe\n' now fails validation.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: re.search(r'^[\w.@+-]+$', 'joe\n') matches because $ tolerates one final newline; with \Z there is no match, so RegexValidator raises ValidationError. This is exactly the reported bug and the minimal fix.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: No change needed (verify by running 'joe\n' through both validators before and after the patch).

requiredFindings.summary: The character class and the ^ start anchor are unchanged; the only behavioral change is rejecting values that matched solely because of $'s newline tolerance.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: Without re.MULTILINE, ^ already anchors at the true string start and [\w.@+-]+ is untouched, so every previously valid username still passes; there is no regression for existing users.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: No change needed (spot-check normal usernames such as 'joe', 'jean-marc', 'René' against both validators).

requiredFindings.summary: Both ASCIIUsernameValidator and UnicodeUsernameValidator get the same anchor change, and each gains a trailing-newline entry in the invalid_usernames regression tests.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: The two classes share the same pattern and Unicode is the default validator; fixing only one would leave the bug half-open. The new entries assert ValidationError and fail on the old regex, so they are trustworthy regression tests.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: No change needed (confirm both regex changes and both test additions are present).

optionalFindings.summary: Style nit: \A would mirror \Z at the start of the pattern, but ^ is equivalent here because re.MULTILINE is not set; not a blocker.

disallowedConclusions.description: Reject because \Z still permits a trailing newline — that is Perl/PCRE \Z semantics; Python's \Z matches only at the absolute end of the string (like \z elsewhere).
Reject because the input should be stripped/normalized instead of rejected — a validator must judge the submitted value, not silently rewrite it.
Reject because ^ must also be changed to \A to make the fix complete — without re.MULTILINE the two are equivalent, so this is a style preference, not a blocker.
